---
# ! Render this project via `targets::tar_make()` (the `render_manuscript`
# ! target in _targets.R), not `quarto render` on this file directly — see
# ! manuscript.qmd.
title: "How a .qmd becomes html/docx/pdf: the compute → filter → convert pipeline"
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(here)
here::i_am("manuscript/supplementary/rendering-pipeline.qmd")
```

{{< include _back-to-manuscript.qmd >}}

::: {.callout-tip}
# The Short Version
- One `.qmd` is executed **once** by knitr, producing a single intermediate
  markdown document with computed output already substituted in — R only
  runs once per render, not once per output format.
- That intermediate markdown then goes through Quarto's Lua filters
  (cross-reference numbering, this project's `flextable-qmd` filter), and
  only *then* to pandoc, which has a separate writer per output format.
- docx and typst are not "pandoc understands Word/PDF directly" — docx is
  pandoc writing OOXML text with **named style references**, and typst is
  pandoc writing Typst *markup*, which Quarto's bundled typst compiler then
  compiles to PDF as a distinct final step.
- The docx path is the one with a sharp edge: pandoc tags text with a style
  *name* (`Hyperlink`, `Table`, `Strong`, ...) and leaves the actual
  formatting up to whatever `reference-doc` defines for that name. A name
  Word lists as available but never actually defines (a "latent" style)
  silently renders as plain text — no error, no warning. This is exactly
  what was wrong with hyperlinks in this template until it was fixed
  alongside this notebook — see the worked example below.
:::

# Why this notebook exists

Everything else under `supplementary/` is a minimal reproducible example —
render this content, look at the file, report what actually happened
([tables-mre.qmd](tables-mre.qmd), [images-mre.qmd](images-mre.qmd)). This
notebook is a bit different: it exists to explain a piece of *mechanism*
that's easy to work around without ever understanding, right up until it
breaks in a way that only makes sense once you know how the pieces fit
together. It also plays double duty as the writeup for a real bug found
and fixed while writing it (the docx hyperlink styling issue), since that
bug is a direct, concrete illustration of the mechanism.

# The pipeline, in one diagram

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-pipeline-overview
%%| fig-cap: "One .qmd, one knitr execution, many pandoc writers"
flowchart TD
    A[".qmd source<br/>prose + R code chunks"] --> B["knitr executes R chunks<br/>(cached by execute: freeze)"]
    B --> C["intermediate markdown<br/>chunk output already substituted in"]
    C --> D["Quarto Lua filters walk the AST<br/>e.g. crossref numbering,<br/>this project's flextable-qmd filter"]
    D --> E{"pandoc — one writer<br/>per target format"}
    E -->|html writer| F["manuscript.html"]
    E -->|docx writer| G["OOXML docx<br/>styled via reference-doc"]
    E -->|typst writer| H["manuscript.typ<br/>(Typst markup, not a PDF yet)"]
    H --> I["Quarto's bundled typst compiler"]
    I --> J["manuscript.pdf"]
```

Four stages, each confirmed by direct testing in this project rather than
assumed from documentation:

## 1. Compute — knitr runs once, not once per format

For an R-engine `.qmd` (the default in this `website`-type project — see
[CLAUDE.md](../../CLAUDE.md)'s "Core architectural decision"), knitr
executes every code chunk and produces **one** intermediate markdown
document with computed output already inlined — figures rendered to image
files and referenced, table objects printed, inline R expressions
evaluated. This happens once per `.qmd`, before pandoc sees anything, and
before Quarto even knows it needs to hand off to three different writers.

This is also exactly what `execute: freeze` controls
(`manuscript/_quarto.yml`): it's a cache over *this* stage specifically —
skip re-running knitr and reuse the last intermediate markdown. It has
nothing to do with pandoc or the output formats downstream, which is why
`freeze: true` can serve stale *content* indefinitely (a real regression
hit earlier in this project, see CLAUDE.md's "Related, separate gotcha")
while having zero effect on, say, a docx styling problem — that lives
entirely in stage 3, described below.

## 2. Filter — Lua filters walk the pandoc AST before conversion

Before any format-specific writer runs, Quarto passes the document through
Lua filters that operate on pandoc's internal document representation (its
abstract syntax tree, not text). Two examples already active in this
project's `manuscript/_quarto.yml`:

- Quarto's own crossref filter, which turns `@fig-example` / `@tbl-example`
  / `@eq-poisson` into numbered, format-appropriate links (`Figure 1`,
  `Table 1`, ...) — confirmed below to matter for the hyperlink styling
  bug, since crossrefs render as real hyperlinks in docx, not just plain
  text with a number.
- This project's own `filters: [flextable-qmd]` line, which is what makes
  the flextable-based tables in [tables-mre.qmd](tables-mre.qmd) work
  identically across formats.

## 3. Convert — pandoc, one writer per format, docx via named styles

This is the stage where html, docx, and typst genuinely diverge, and where
they diverge *differently* than you'd guess:

- **html**: pandoc writes HTML tags directly (`<a>`, `<table>`, ...) and
  the browser's own default styling (plus `styles/styles.css`) does the
  rest. A hyperlink is blue and underlined here for free — that's just
  what browsers do with `<a>` tags with no extra work from pandoc or this
  project.
- **docx**: pandoc does **not** hand-craft formatting into the OOXML runs
  it writes. Instead it tags spans of text with a **style reference** —
  `<w:rStyle w:val="Hyperlink"/>` for link text, similarly for `Strong`,
  `Table`, and others already documented in CLAUDE.md — and leaves the
  actual color/weight/border/etc. to be looked up from whatever
  `reference-doc` supplies for a style with that exact name. This is the
  same relationship as an HTML `class="..."` attribute and an external
  CSS file: the tag alone renders nothing.
- **typst**: pandoc writes **Typst markup** (`manuscript.typ`) — a distinct
  intermediate artifact, confirmed directly by watching a render log:
  `[typst]: Compiling manuscript.typ to manuscript.pdf...DONE`. This is pandoc's
  typst *writer*, functionally the same kind of thing as its docx or html
  writer, just targeting a different output language.

## 4. Compile — only typst has this extra step

html and docx are pandoc's actual final output — nothing further happens
to them. typst is different: pandoc's output (`manuscript.typ`) is source code
in the Typst language, and Quarto's bundled Typst compiler (no separate
system install needed — confirmed in CLAUDE.md) compiles *that* into
`manuscript.pdf`. A typst-stage failure is therefore a compile error in
generated Typst markup, not a pandoc conversion error — which is also why
one bad image format can hard-fail typst specifically while html/docx
sail through unaffected (documented in [images-mre.qmd](images-mre.qmd)'s
TIFF section): the failure is happening one stage later than it is for
the other two formats.

# Worked example: the docx hyperlink styling gap

This project had a real instance of the stage-3 failure mode described
above: hyperlinks rendered as working links in the docx output (clicking
them navigated correctly) but displayed as plain black, non-underlined
text — nothing visually marked them as links, unlike html where this is
automatic.

## Confirming the mechanism, not guessing at it

Generating a one-line test document and inspecting the raw OOXML pandoc
produced showed exactly what stage 3 above predicts:

```xml
<w:hyperlink r:id="rId20">
  <w:r>
    <w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Hyperlink" /></w:rPr>
    <w:t xml:space="preserve">link to example</w:t>
  </w:r>
</w:hyperlink>
```

Pandoc *is* tagging the run with `w:rStyle val="Hyperlink"` unconditionally
— this part was never the problem. Checking both `styles/db-space-line-n.docx`
and `styles/standard.docx` (this project's two reference docs) for an
actual definition of that style turned up nothing:

```xml
<!-- word/styles.xml in both reference docs had: -->
<w:lsdException w:name="Hyperlink" w:semiHidden="1" w:unhideWhenUsed="1"/>
<!--
  i.e. Word knows "Hyperlink" as a style NAME it could show in the
  style gallery, but no <w:style w:styleId="Hyperlink"> block anywhere
  actually defines what it looks like.
-->
```

That `<w:lsdException>` entry is a **latent style declaration** — Word's
way of saying "this name is reserved/known, offer it in the UI if asked,"
which is a different thing from a style *definition*
(`<w:style w:type="character" w:styleId="Hyperlink">...<w:rPr>...`). Pandoc
references the name; nothing in either reference doc backed it with actual
formatting. This is the identical failure shape CLAUDE.md already documents
for the `Table` style — "missing style falls back to a generic default,
Word does not warn you" — just discovered independently for `Hyperlink`.

## The fix

A character style definition was added to both reference docs, based on
Word's own built-in default for this exact style name:

```xml
<w:style w:type="character" w:styleId="Hyperlink">
  <w:name w:val="Hyperlink"/>
  <w:basedOn w:val="DefaultParagraphFont"/>
  <w:uiPriority w:val="99"/>
  <w:unhideWhenUsed/>
  <w:rPr>
    <w:color w:val="0563C1"/>
    <w:u w:val="single"/>
  </w:rPr>
</w:style>
```

Confirmed end-to-end, not just at the level of the reference doc in
isolation: a full `targets::tar_make()` rebuild of this project, followed
by extracting `manuscript/output/manuscript.docx`'s own `word/document.xml` and
`word/styles.xml` directly, showed **six** hyperlink runs in the real
manuscript — cross-references to an equation, a section, a figure, and a
table, plus a citation link — all tagged `w:rStyle val="Hyperlink"`, and
the now-present style definition sitting in the output docx's own
`styles.xml`. Every one of those was previously unstyled in Word, not just
plain external URLs; Quarto's crossref filter (stage 2 above) renders
cross-references as real docx hyperlinks, so the bug silently affected
in-document navigation too, not only links to outside URLs.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-hyperlink-sequence
%%| fig-cap: "Why an undefined style renders silently instead of erroring"
sequenceDiagram
    participant MD as intermediate markdown/AST
    participant PW as pandoc docx writer
    participant RD as reference-doc styles.xml
    participant W as Word

    MD->>PW: "[link text](url)" / a crossref
    PW->>PW: wrap the run in w:rStyle val="Hyperlink"
    PW->>RD: look up styleId="Hyperlink"
    alt style actually defined
        RD-->>PW: color + underline copied into output docx
        PW->>W: styled blue, underlined text
    else style only latent (name known, never defined)
        RD-->>PW: nothing to copy - no error raised
        PW->>W: falls back to Normal - no color, no warning
    end
```

## The general recipe this generalizes to

Any "it renders, but looks wrong specifically in Word, specifically not in
html" symptom is worth checking against this same mechanism before
suspecting anything more exotic:

1. Generate a minimal doc containing the construct in question and convert
   it with pandoc directly (bypassing Quarto/targets, just to isolate the
   stage), then extract `word/document.xml` and look for the `w:rStyle` /
   `w:pStyle` name pandoc chose.
2. Check whether that exact style name is *defined* (has real `<w:rPr>` /
   `<w:pPr>` content), not just *listed*, in whichever `reference-doc` is
   configured in `manuscript/_quarto.yml`.
3. If it's missing or only latent, add a real `<w:style>` block for that
   name — matching Word's own built-in default is usually the least
   surprising choice, as done above for `Hyperlink`.

CLAUDE.md's "The Word reference doc" section lists the other style names
pandoc is already confirmed to reference this way (`Table`,
`TableCaption`, `BodyText`, `Compact`, ...) — the same check is worth
running against any of those if a new formatting gap turns up.

# References
